name: Maltodextrin
profile: An ultra-processed carbohydrate powder used as a thickener and bulk filler. It has a massive glycemic index of 110, which spikes blood glucose levels significantly faster than standard table sugar.

name: Aspartame
profile: A low-calorie artificial sweetener found in diet sodas and sugar-free foods. It is studied for potential neurotoxic effects, chronic headaches, disruptions to gut microbiome flora, and insulin sensitivity anomalies.

name: Monosodium Glutamate (MSG)
profile: An industrial sodium salt used to artificially intensify savory umami flavors. In sensitive individuals, it can trigger acute neurological symptoms including cluster headaches, flushing, sweating, and localized facial pressure.

name: High Fructose Corn Syrup (HFCS)
profile: A liquid sweetener alternative derived from heavily chemically altered corn starch. It bypasses regular digestive checkpoints and processes entirely in the liver, drastically increasing the risks of non-alcoholic fatty liver disease and insulin resistance.

name: Sodium Lauryl Sulfate (SLS)
profile: A harsh chemical foaming surfactant commonly found in commercial shampoos, body washes, and toothpastes. It aggressively strips the skin of its natural protective lipids, frequently resulting in severe contact dermatitis, scaling, and eczema flare-ups.

name: Methylparaben
profile: An endocrine-disrupting chemical preservative utilized to prolong the shelf life of creams, lotions, and makeup. It mimics estrogen hormones within the human body and has been found embedded in biopsied breast tumor tissues.

name: DMDM Hydantoin
profile: A chemical preservative found in shampoos, conditioners, and liquid soaps that slowly off-gasses small amounts of formaldehyde over time to prevent consumer product spoilage. It is a classified human carcinogen and a severe immune system allergen.

name: TBHQ (Tertiary Butylhydroquinone)
profile: A heavily used synthetic antioxidant in Indian packaged snacks (bhujia, chips) and refined oils to extend shelf life. Linked in some studies to immune system suppression and neurological issues.

name: Tartrazine (E102)
profile: A bright yellow synthetic dye widely used in Indian sweets (jalebi, laddoos) and savory snacks. Known to cause allergic reactions and linked to hyperactivity in children.

name: Sunset Yellow FCF (E110)
profile: An orange food dye commonly found in Indian squash, sherbets, and packaged namkeen. Suspected of contributing to behavioral issues and allergic intolerance.

name: Palmolein Oil
profile: A highly refined, cheap derivative of palm oil ubiquitous in Indian packaged foods and street food. High consumption is linked to cardiovascular disease due to its high saturated fat content and processing methods.

name: Vanaspati (Hydrogenated Vegetable Oil)
profile: A cheap ghee substitute widely used in Indian commercial baking and sweet making. It is a major source of artificial trans fats, heavily linked to heart disease and arterial plaque.

name: Disodium Inosinate (E631) & Guanylate (E627)
profile: Often used alongside MSG in instant noodles (like Maggi) and 'magic masala' spice mixes to create a highly addictive savory taste. Can trigger symptoms in sensitive individuals.

name: Carmoisine (E122)
profile: A synthetic red dye commonly used in Indian jellies, jams, and flavored milk. Banned in some countries due to its potential to cause allergic reactions and hyperactivity.

name: Ponceau 4R (E124)
profile: A strawberry red azo dye used in Indian sweets and canned fruits. Considered a potential carcinogen in some animal studies and known to trigger asthma-like symptoms.

name: Sodium Metabisulphite (E223)
profile: Used heavily in Indian dried fruits, dehydrated vegetables, and juices to preserve color. Can cause severe asthmatic reactions and digestive distress in sensitive people.

name: Potassium Sorbate (E202)
profile: Widely used in Indian packaged paneer, sauces, and baked goods to prevent mold. Generally considered safe, but synthetic forms can cause skin and eye irritation in factory workers or sensitive consumers.

name: Sodium Benzoate (E211)
profile: Extremely common in Indian pickles (achar), sauces, and carbonated drinks. When combined with Vitamin C, it can form benzene, a known carcinogen.

name: Caramel Color (E150c / E150d)
profile: Used in Indian colas, soy sauce, and some dark sweets. Classes III and IV contain 4-MEI, a byproduct classified as a possible human carcinogen by the IARC.

name: Liquid Glucose / Invert Sugar
profile: Cheap sugar syrups heavily used in Indian commercial biscuits and sweets. Drives rapid spikes in blood sugar and contributes strongly to insulin resistance and metabolic syndrome.

name: Ajinomoto (Monosodium Glutamate)
profile: Ubiquitous in Indo-Chinese fast food and packaged soups. Overconsumption leads to 'Chinese Restaurant Syndrome'—headaches, sweating, and chest pain in sensitive individuals.

name: Potassium Bromate (E924)
profile: Historically used in Indian pav (bread) and commercial bakery items to improve dough elasticity. It is a classified possible human carcinogen, leading to bans or heavy restrictions.

name: Calcium Propionate (E282)
profile: A standard preservative in packaged Indian breads (sliced bread, pav, buns) to prevent mold. Some studies link high intake to irritability and sleep disturbances in children.

name: Silver Leaf (Vark) - Adulterated
profile: Used to decorate Indian sweets (mithai). While pure silver is safe, cheap vark in local markets is frequently adulterated with toxic aluminum or heavy metals.

name: Brilliant Blue FCF (E133)
profile: Used in Indian blue-colored candies, mouth fresheners (mukhwas), and beverages. Can cause hypersensitivity reactions and is poorly absorbed by the digestive tract.

name: Saccharin (E954)
profile: Often found in cheap Indian diet sweets and diabetic-friendly items. Early animal studies linked it to bladder cancer, though later downgraded, it remains controversial and alters gut flora.

name: Sucralose (E955)
profile: Used in 'sugar-free' Indian sweets and diet drinks. Recent studies show it can drastically reduce beneficial gut bacteria and may impact insulin sensitivity despite having zero calories.

name: Titanium Dioxide (E171)
profile: Used to make Indian sweets, chewing gums, and pan masala appear bright white. The EU banned it as a food additive due to concerns over genotoxicity (DNA damage).

name: Annatto (E160b)
profile: A natural orange-red dye used in Indian butter, cheese, and snacks. While natural, it is a known allergen that can cause hives and irritable bowel symptoms in sensitive individuals.

name: Carrageenan (E407)
profile: Extracted from red seaweed, used in Indian flavored milks, ice creams, and dairy alternatives. Degraded carrageenan is linked to severe gastrointestinal inflammation and ulcers.

name: Guar Gum (E412)
profile: Native to India, used widely in ice creams and sauces. Safe in normal amounts, but high concentrations in processed foods can cause significant bloating and digestive gas.

name: Polysorbate 80 (E433)
profile: Used in Indian ice creams to prevent melting. Animal studies indicate it can alter gut microbiota, leading to low-grade inflammation and promoting obesity and metabolic syndrome.

name: Sodium Stearoyl Lactylate (E481)
profile: A dough conditioner common in Indian commercial baked goods to keep bread soft. Heavily processed and contributes to the ultra-processed nature of modern staple breads.

name: Diacetyl
profile: The chemical that gives 'butter flavor' to Indian microwave popcorn and cheap baked goods. Inhalation by factory workers causes 'popcorn lung,' a severe irreversible lung disease.

name: Malt Extract
profile: Used in Indian health drinks (like Bournvita, Horlicks) and biscuits. Often acts as a hidden sugar source, marketed as 'healthy' but driving significant caloric intake.

name: Cottonseed Oil
profile: Often used as a cheap adulterant or frying oil in local Indian snacks. Unless highly refined, it contains gossypol, a natural toxin, and is heavily treated with pesticides.

name: Erythrosine (E127)
profile: A cherry-pink synthetic dye used in Indian sweets, cherries, and canned fruits. It is an iodine-containing dye linked to thyroid tumors in animal testing.

name: Sulphur Dioxide (E220)
profile: Used to bleach and preserve Indian jaggery (gur) and dried fruits. High residues can trigger severe asthma attacks and degrade vitamin B1 in the body.

name: Phosphoric Acid (E338)
profile: Provides the tangy bite in Indian colas. High consumption interferes with calcium absorption, leading to lower bone density and increased risk of osteoporosis.

name: Sodium Nitrite (E250)
profile: Used in processed meats (sausages, salamis) increasingly popular in urban India. Cooking at high heat forms nitrosamines, which are potent, known carcinogens.

name: Artificial Vanilla Flavor (Vanillin)
profile: A synthetic substitute for real vanilla, heavily used in Indian biscuits and ice creams. Often derived from petrochemicals or wood pulp processing byproducts.

name: Shellac (E904)
profile: A resin secreted by the female lac bug, used to give a shiny coating to Indian apples, citrus fruits, and hard candies. Often not disclosed to vegetarian consumers.

name: Carnauba Wax (E903)
profile: Used alongside shellac to coat fruits and sweets in India. While generally safe, it prevents the washing away of pesticides trapped underneath the wax layer.

name: BVO (Brominated Vegetable Oil)
profile: Historically used in citrus-flavored sodas in India to keep flavor oils suspended. Bromine builds up in the body and can cause memory loss, skin issues, and nerve problems.

name: Azodicarbonamide (E927a)
profile: The 'yoga mat chemical' used to bleach and condition flour in commercial bakeries. Banned in many countries as it breaks down into urethane, a recognized carcinogen.

name: High Maltose Corn Syrup
profile: A cheaper alternative to sugar used in Indian candies and jams. Rapidly spikes blood sugar, contributing to the growing epidemic of Type 2 Diabetes in India.

name: Propyl Gallate (E310)
profile: Often used in conjunction with BHA/BHT in Indian meat products and oil-rich snacks to prevent spoilage. Suspected of being an endocrine disruptor.

name: Paraphenylenediamine (PPD)
profile: Extremely common in cheap Indian black hair dyes and 'black mehndi'. A potent allergen causing severe facial swelling, blistering, and long-term immunological sensitization.

name: Hydroquinone
profile: The active ingredient in many Indian skin-whitening and fairness creams. Prolonged use causes ochronosis (blue-black skin discoloration) and it is heavily restricted globally.

name: Mercury
profile: Illegally present in some unregulated, cheap fairness creams sold in local Indian markets. Highly toxic, causing kidney damage, skin scarring, and severe neurological issues.

name: Lead (in Surma / Kohl)
profile: Traditional Indian eye cosmetics (surma) often contain dangerously high levels of lead sulfide. Absorbed through the eyes, it causes irreversible neurological damage, especially in children.

name: Coal Tar
profile: Found in some Indian anti-dandruff shampoos and dark hair dyes. A known human carcinogen derived from petroleum processing.

name: Toluene
profile: Common in cheap Indian nail polishes and nail polish removers. Inhalation affects the central nervous system, causing headaches, dizziness, and reproductive harm.

name: Resorcinol
profile: Used frequently in Indian chemical hair dyes. It is a known endocrine disruptor affecting thyroid function and a frequent cause of scalp dermatitis.

name: Triclocarban
profile: Used in Indian antibacterial bar soaps (like certain health soaps). Like Triclosan, it disrupts hormones and persists in the environment, breeding superbugs.

name: Talc (Contaminated)
profile: Widely used in Indian prickly heat powders and baby powders. Unpurified cosmetic talc is frequently contaminated with asbestos, a deadly carcinogen linked to mesothelioma.

name: DEA (Diethanolamine)
profile: Used in Indian shampoos and body washes to create a rich lather. Can react with other ingredients to form nitrosodiethanolamine (NDEA), a potent carcinogen absorbed through the skin.

name: Refined Wheat Flour (Maida)
profile: The primary base for most Indian biscuits. Highly refined, stripped of fiber and nutrients, leading to rapid blood sugar spikes and empty calories.

name: Invert Sugar Syrup
profile: A liquid sweetener commonly used in Indian biscuits to retain moisture and control texture. Like high fructose corn syrup, high intake contributes to insulin resistance and obesity.

name: Ammonium Bicarbonate
profile: Used to make biscuits rise and give them a porous, crisp texture. Generally recognized as safe (GRAS), it degrades into ammonia and carbon dioxide during baking, leaving little residue.

name: Soya Lecithin
profile: Extracted from soybeans, used in biscuits and chocolates to blend oil and water. Safe for most, but can be an allergen for those highly sensitive to soy. Often derived from GMO soy.

name: Gram Flour (Besan)
profile: A staple, protein-rich flour made from ground chickpeas. Safe, nutritious, and naturally gluten-free, forming the base of bhujia and sev.

name: Citric Acid (INS 330)
profile: Used in namkeen to provide a tangy 'khatta' flavor and act as a mild preservative. Usually manufactured synthetically using black mold (Aspergillus niger). Safe in moderation.

name: Silicon Dioxide (INS 551)
profile: Added to spice mixes ('masala') in namkeen to prevent clumping. Though naturally occurring as sand/quartz, the nanoparticles used in food are debated regarding long-term gut health impacts.

name: Sodium Fluoride
profile: The primary active ingredient in commercial toothpastes. Essential for remineralizing enamel, but highly toxic if swallowed in large quantities (fluorosis).

name: Calcium Carbonate
profile: A mild abrasive used in toothpastes to scrub away plaque and surface stains. Found in both commercial and Ayurvedic toothpastes. Safe for oral use.

name: Hydrated Silica
profile: Another common abrasive. While effective at cleaning, overly aggressive brushing with highly abrasive silica toothpastes can wear down enamel over time.

name: Sorbitol
profile: Keeps toothpaste from drying out in the tube and adds a sweet taste. Safe, though consuming large amounts (e.g., in sugar-free gums) has a laxative effect.

name: Sodium Lauryl Sulphate (SLS)
profile: Creates the foaming action in almost all Indian toothpastes. A known severe irritant that frequently causes or exacerbates mouth ulcers (canker sores) in sensitive individuals.

name: Cellulose Gum
profile: A plant-derived thickening agent used to give toothpaste its creamy texture. Completely safe and non-toxic.

name: Sodium Saccharin
profile: Used to mask the bitter taste of cleaning agents in toothpaste. An artificial zero-calorie sweetener; generally safe in the tiny amounts absorbed orally.

name: Arginine
profile: An amino acid sometimes added to premium toothpastes to help neutralize plaque acids and block dentin tubules, reducing tooth sensitivity.

name: Sodium Palmate
profile: The chemical salt resulting from the reaction of palm oil with lye. The primary cleansing agent in almost all Indian bar soaps. Safe, but strips natural skin oils, causing dryness.

name: Sodium Palm Kernelate
profile: Derived from palm kernel oil. Works alongside Sodium Palmate to create a hard soap bar with a rich, bubbly lather. Also contributes to stripping skin moisture.

name: Glycerin
profile: A natural byproduct of the soap-making process. Draws moisture from the air into the skin. Highly beneficial, though commercial soaps often extract it to sell separately.

name: Tetrasodium EDTA
profile: Added to soaps to bind to hard water minerals, ensuring the soap lathers well in all water types. Safe for humans, but poses severe environmental persistence concerns.

name: Etidronic Acid
profile: Used in bar soaps to prevent discoloration and rancidity caused by metal ions. Generally safe, but can be a mild skin irritant for those with eczema.

name: Silver Oxide
profile: Used in 'Activ Silver' germ-protection soaps. Effective at disrupting bacterial cell walls. While safe topically, environmental accumulation of nanosilver is a growing concern.

name: Chloroxylenol (PCMX)
profile: The classic active ingredient in Dettol products. A potent antiseptic and disinfectant. Safe for human skin in low concentrations, but highly toxic to cats and fish.

name: Climbazole
profile: An antifungal agent found in some anti-dandruff shampoos and specialized soaps. Effective against yeast-like fungi, but restricted in some regions due to potential endocrine disruption.

name: Thymol
profile: A naturally occurring biocide found in thyme oil, often used in medicated soaps and mouthwashes for its strong antibacterial and antifungal properties.

name: Terpineol
profile: Found in pine oil, it is used in antiseptic soaps for its characteristic medicinal scent and mild bactericidal properties.

name: PGPR (E476)
profile: Polyglycerol Polyricinoleate. A cheap chemical emulsifier used in Indian chocolates (like Dairy Milk) to replace expensive cocoa butter. Derived from castor beans; generally safe but heavily processed.

name: Ammonium Phosphatides (E442)
profile: Used alongside PGPR in chocolate manufacturing to alter the thickness of chocolate. A synthetic alternative to soy lecithin.

name: Nisin (E234)
profile: A polycyclic antibacterial peptide used in packaged paneer and cheese slices in India to prevent spoilage. Safe and naturally derived from lactococcus lactis.

name: Sodium Alginate (E401)
profile: Extracted from brown seaweed, used to thicken Indian flavored milks and ice creams. Generally safe, but can inhibit the absorption of iron and calcium if consumed in massive quantities.

name: Xanthan Gum (E415)
profile: Created through bacterial fermentation, used in Indian sauces, ketchups, and gluten-free products to prevent ingredient separation. Completely safe.

name: Sodium Phosphates (E339)
profile: Used in processed cheese and evaporated milk. High consumption of inorganic phosphates can negatively impact kidney health and bone density.

name: Disodium Ribonucleotides (E635)
profile: Often paired with MSG in Indian instant noodles and chips. Can trigger gout or worsen symptoms in sensitive individuals by increasing uric acid levels.

name: Malic Acid (E296)
profile: Provides a tart, sour taste to Indian fruit juices and sour candies. Safe, naturally occurs in apples, though commercial forms are synthetically produced.

name: Paprika Oleoresin (E160c)
profile: A natural orange-red extract from bell peppers used in spicy Indian namkeen and sauces. Safe and non-toxic.

name: Calcium Silicate (E552)
profile: Used in Indian table salt and spice powders (garam masala, chaat masala) to prevent them from absorbing moisture and clumping. Safe.

name: Light Liquid Paraffin (Mineral Oil)
profile: Extremely common in commercial Indian 'Amla' or 'Almond' hair oils. It is a highly refined crude oil derivative that coats the hair, blocking moisture absorption. Cheaper than real vegetable oils.

name: Dimethicone
profile: A synthetic silicone used in hair serums and conditioners to create artificial shine and slip. It causes severe product buildup over time, requiring harsh sulfates to remove.

name: Menthol & Camphor
profile: Used in 'cooling' Ayurvedic hair oils (like Navratna) to provide a tingling, cooling sensation. Safe for adults, but can be highly irritating to broken skin or the eyes.

name: Amla Extract (Phyllanthus Emblica)
profile: Indian gooseberry extract. Extremely rich in Vitamin C and antioxidants, highly beneficial for hair follicle strength and scalp health.

name: Mineral Oil (Paraffinum Liquidum)
profile: Used in almost all cheap baby oils and body lotions in India. While it seals in moisture, it provides zero nutrients to the skin and can trap dirt, leading to breakouts.

name: Niacinamide
profile: Vitamin B3. Highly popular in Indian face creams and serums. Exceptionally safe and effective for brightening skin, reducing redness, and strengthening the skin barrier.

name: Octinoxate
profile: Used in Indian fairness creams with SPF. A known endocrine disruptor that mimics estrogen in the body and is highly damaging to marine coral reefs.

name: Cetyl Alcohol
profile: Unlike drying alcohols, this is a waxy, hydrating 'fatty' alcohol used to thicken lotions and creams. Safe and non-irritating.

name: Stearic Acid
profile: A natural fatty acid found in plant and animal fats, used in Indian vanishing creams and shaving creams to bind water and oil together. Completely safe.

name: Propylparaben & Butylparaben
profile: Used to extend the shelf life of heavy creams. More highly linked to endocrine (hormone) disruption than shorter-chain parabens. Banned in some countries.

name: Kojic Acid
profile: A natural byproduct of fermenting malting rice, used in Indian skin-lightening soaps and creams as a safer alternative to hydroquinone. Safe, but can cause sun sensitivity.

name: BHT (Butylated Hydroxytoluene)
profile: Used in hair oils and lipsticks to prevent the oils from going rancid. Suspected endocrine disruptor and linked to organ-system toxicity in high doses.

name: Phenoxyethanol
profile: The most common replacement for parabens in modern 'paraben-free' Indian cosmetics. Safe in concentrations under 1%, but can be irritating to infants and highly sensitive skin.

name: Fragrance (Parfum / Sugandhit Dravya)
profile: A loophole term used on almost every Indian cosmetic label. 'Fragrance' can legally hide hundreds of undisclosed synthetic chemicals, including toxic phthalates.

name: Water
profile: The universal solvent and base for most food and cosmetic products. Completely safe and essential for hydration.

name: Aqua
profile: The INCI (cosmetic) naming convention for purified water. Completely safe and forms the base of most lotions and shampoos.

name: Aloe Vera
profile: A natural plant extract highly prized for its soothing, anti-inflammatory, and hydrating properties. Completely safe.

name: Coconut Oil
profile: A natural edible oil extracted from coconuts. Used in cooking and cosmetics for its rich moisturizing and antimicrobial properties. Completely safe.

name: Sunflower Oil
profile: A light, non-comedogenic oil pressed from sunflower seeds. Rich in Vitamin E and safe for both consumption and topical application.

name: Shea Butter
profile: A fat extracted from the nut of the African shea tree. Highly moisturizing and widely used in premium lotions and lip balms. Completely safe.

name: Flavour enhancer 635 (Disodium 5'-ribonucleotides)
profile: A food additive combining disodium inosinate and disodium guanylate, used to create a synergistic umami effect. May trigger gout or skin rashes in sensitive individuals.

name: Flavour enhancer 621 (Monosodium Glutamate / MSG)
profile: Sodium salt of glutamic acid used to intensify savory flavors. Can cause sensitivity symptoms like headaches, flushing, or sweating in sensitive individuals.

name: Flavour enhancer 627 (Disodium Guanylate)
profile: A flavor enhancer often paired with MSG. Metabolizes into purines, so individuals with gout or uric acid kidney stones should limit consumption.

name: Flavour enhancer 631 (Disodium Inosinate)
profile: A flavor enhancer used in instant noodles, potato chips, and savory snacks. Often combined with monosodium glutamate to boost umami taste.

name: Yeast Extract
profile: A natural flavor enhancer derived from yeast cells. Rich in glutamic acid (natural MSG), used to provide rich savory flavors to processed foods.

name: Hydrolyzed Vegetable Protein (HVP)
profile: A flavor enhancer created by boiling soy, corn, or wheat in hydrochloric acid. Contains free glutamic acid and can trigger MSG-like sensitivities.

name: Hydrolysed Groundnut Protein
profile: Peanut protein chemically broken down into amino acids. Used for flavoring, but presents severe allergy risks for individuals sensitive to peanuts.

name: Textured Vegetable Protein (TVP)
profile: A soy protein derivative used as a meat analogue. Rich in protein and generally safe, though heavily processed.

name: Autolyzed Yeast
profile: Yeast that has been allowed to self-digest using its own enzymes. Used as a natural flavoring agent rich in savory glutamic acids.

name: Soy Protein Isolate
profile: A highly refined soy protein powder. Safe for consumption, though highly processed and a common marker of industrial food products.

name: Colour 150d (Sulfite ammonia caramel)
profile: A dark brown coloring agent produced by heating sugars in the presence of sulfites and ammonium compounds. Can contain 4-MEI, a potential carcinogen in high doses.

name: Colour 150a (Plain caramel)
profile: A brown food coloring made by heating carbohydrates under controlled conditions. Generally considered safe and free of sulfite/ammonium compounds.

name: Colour 102 (Tartrazine / Yellow 5)
profile: A synthetic yellow azo dye used in foods and drugs. Linked to hyperactive behavior in children and allergic reactions, especially in aspirin-sensitive individuals.

name: Colour 110 (Sunset Yellow FCF / Yellow 6)
profile: A synthetic orange dye used in squashes, sweets, and snacks. Associated with allergic reactions and behavioral changes in sensitive children.

name: Colour 129 (Allura Red AC / Red 40)
profile: A widely used synthetic red food dye. Studied for minor hypersensitivity links and behavioral issues in children.

name: Colour 133 (Brilliant Blue FCF / Blue 1)
profile: A synthetic blue dye used in confectioneries and dairy products. Generally safe in small amounts, though minor hypersensitivity has been reported.

name: Colour 127 (Erythrosine / Red 3)
profile: A synthetic cherry-pink food dye. Banned in many countries for food use due to its link to thyroid tumors and endocrine disruption in animal studies.

name: Colour 171 (Titanium Dioxide)
profile: A white pigment used in candies, gum, and sunscreens. Banned in the EU as a food additive due to concerns over genotoxicity and accumulation of nanoparticles in the body.

name: Thickener 412 (Guar Gum)
profile: A natural seed gum extract used to thicken foods. Promotes digestive health in low quantities, though high doses can cause gastrointestinal discomfort.

name: Thickener 415 (Xanthan Gum)
profile: A polysaccharide produced through bacterial fermentation. Used as a stabilizing agent. Safe, though large amounts can cause digestive issues.

name: Thickener 407 (Carrageenan)
profile: An seaweed extract used to thicken dairy and plant-based milks. Subject to debate over potential to cause gut inflammation and digestive irritation.

name: Thickener 466 (Sodium Carboxymethyl Cellulose)
profile: A chemically modified cellulose gum used to thicken foods. May alter gut bacteria composition and trigger mild digestive irritation in large doses.

name: Stabilizer 170 (Calcium Carbonate)
profile: A dietary mineral used as a firming agent, stabilizer, or white colorant. Safe for consumption and serves as a calcium source.

name: Thickener 508 (Potassium Chloride)
profile: A naturally occurring mineral salt used as a gelling agent and sodium substitute. Safe for general consumption, but should be moderated by individuals with kidney issues.

name: Edible Starch
profile: Natural carbohydrate extract from plants (corn, wheat, potato) used to thicken food textures. Completely safe but high in carbohydrates.

name: Modified Corn Starch
profile: Corn starch physically or chemically altered to withstand extreme processing temperatures. Safe, though highly processed.

name: Dextrin
profile: A low-molecular-weight carbohydrate produced by the hydrolysis of starch. Used as a crisping agent, adhesive, and thickener. Safe for consumption.

name: Acidity regulator 330 (Citric Acid)
profile: A weak organic acid naturally found in citrus fruits, widely produced industrially. Safe and effective at regulating tartness and preserving freshness.

name: Acidity regulator 500i (Sodium Carbonate)
profile: An inorganic sodium salt used to control pH and raise dough. Safe for consumption.

name: Acidity regulator 500ii (Sodium Bicarbonate / Baking Soda)
profile: Common baking soda. Used as a leavening agent and pH buffer. Safe and widely used.

name: Acidity regulator 501i (Potassium Carbonate)
profile: A mineral salt used to regulate acidity in cocoa processing and baking. Safe for consumption.

name: Humectant 451i (Sodium Triphosphate)
profile: A sodium phosphate salt used to retain moisture in seafood and meats. Excess consumption of dietary phosphates is linked to kidney stress and cardiovascular concerns.

name: Humectant 422 (Glycerin / Glycerol)
profile: A natural sweet-tasting sugar alcohol used to retain moisture in foods and skincare. Completely safe for general use.

name: Acidity regulator 296 (Malic Acid)
profile: An organic acid naturally present in apples and sour fruits. Safe and used to give foods a sharp, tart taste.

name: Acidity regulator 334 (Tartaric Acid)
profile: A naturally occurring crystalline organic acid found in grapes and bananas. Safe, used to provide a sour bite to confectioneries.

name: Preservative 211 (Sodium Benzoate)
profile: A widely used chemical preservative. Can react with vitamin C (ascorbic acid) to form benzene, a known carcinogen, in highly acidic beverages.

name: Preservative 202 (Potassium Sorbate)
profile: A mild chemical preservative used to prevent mold and yeast growth in food and cosmetics. High safety profile and easily metabolized by the body.

name: Preservative 223 (Sodium Metabisulfite)
profile: An inorganic salt used to preserve dried fruits and wines. Releases sulfur dioxide, which can trigger severe asthma and allergic reactions in sensitive individuals.

name: Preservative 220 (Sulfur Dioxide)
profile: A gas used as a preservative to prevent browning and spoilage in dried fruits and wine. A known allergen that can cause severe respiratory distress in asthmatics.

name: Preservative 250 (Sodium Nitrite)
profile: A preservative used in cured meats like bacon, hot dogs, and ham. Can form carcinogenic nitrosamines in the body during high-heat cooking.

name: Preservative 320 (Butylated Hydroxyanisole / BHA)
profile: A synthetic antioxidant used to preserve fats and oils. Classified as a suspected human carcinogen and endocrine disruptor by several health agencies.

name: Preservative 321 (Butylated Hydroxytoluene / BHT)
profile: A synthetic antioxidant used to prevent rancidity in oils and cereals. Linked to endocrine disruption and organ toxicity in animal studies.

name: Liquid Glucose
profile: A concentrated solution of glucose sugar derived from starch. High glycemic index that causes rapid insulin spikes.

name: Corn Syrup Solids
profile: Dried corn syrup used as a sweetener and thickener in processed foods. Possesses a very high glycemic index and carries high risks of metabolic syndrome.

name: Sweetener 951 (Aspartame)
profile: Zero-calorie artificial sweetener. Studied for metabolic and neurological effects in sensitive individuals.

name: Sweetener 955 (Sucralose)
profile: An organochlorine artificial sweetener. Safe in general, though concerns exist regarding its impact on gut flora when heated.

name: Sweetener 950 (Acesulfame Potassium / Ace-K)
profile: An artificial sweetener often blended with aspartame. Ongoing research is conducted on its metabolic effects.

name: Hydrogenated Vegetable Oil
profile: Vegetable oil treated with hydrogen to make it solid. A primary source of trans fats, which raise bad cholesterol and significantly increase cardiovascular disease risk.

name: Propylene Glycol
profile: A common synthetic humectant and solvent. While generally safe in small quantities, it is a known skin irritant that can trigger contact dermatitis in sensitive individuals.

name: Propylparaben
profile: A chemical preservative widely used in cosmetics. It is a known endocrine disruptor that can mimic estrogen, potentially interfering with reproductive health.

name: Butylparaben
profile: An alkyl paraben preservative. Exhibits stronger estrogenic activity than methylparaben, linked to hormone disruption and reproductive toxicity.

name: Triclosan
profile: An antibacterial agent formerly common in soaps and toothpastes. Linked to thyroid hormone disruption, antibiotic resistance, and environmental toxicity.

name: Oxybenzone
profile: A chemical sunscreen agent. Readily absorbed through the skin, linked to endocrine disruption, and banned in select regions to protect marine coral reefs.

name: Erythritol
profile: A naturally occurring sugar alcohol sweetener. Has zero calories and does not affect blood sugar, though excessive consumption can cause minor digestive bloating.

name: Xylitol
profile: A sugar alcohol used in sugar-free gums and oral care. Promotes dental health and is safe for humans, but is highly toxic and lethal to dogs.

name: Stevia
profile: A natural zero-calorie sweetener extracted from Stevia rebaudiana leaves. Highly safe and popular as a sugar alternative for diabetics.

name: Ascorbic Acid
profile: Pure Vitamin C. Functions as a natural antioxidant, dough conditioner, and dietary nutrient. Safe and effective at preventing food browning.

name: Lactic Acid
profile: An organic acid produced by natural fermentation. Used as an acidity regulator in foods and a gentle exfoliating Alpha Hydroxy Acid (AHA) in cosmetics.

name: Disodium EDTA
profile: A heavy metal chelator used to stabilize cosmetics and foods. Safe in small doses, but can act as a skin penetration enhancer for other harmful chemicals.

name: Diethyl Phthalate
profile: A solvent used to extend the duration of synthetic fragrances. Associated with developmental issues, endocrine disruption, and potential reproductive harm.

name: Sodium Sulfite
profile: An inorganic preservative used in dried foods and beverages. Can trigger severe respiratory reactions and asthma attacks in sulfite-sensitive individuals.

name: Sodium Lauryl Sulfoacetate
profile: A mild, safe biodegradable surfactant derived from coconut oil. Unlike SLS, it is skin-friendly, does not cause severe skin irritation, and is completely safe for sensitive skin.

name: Sodium Cocoyl Isethionate
profile: An exceptionally mild surfactant derived from coconut fatty acids, commonly referred to as baby foam. Gently cleanses the skin without stripping moisture.

name: Cocamidopropyl Betaine
profile: An organic surfactant derived from coconut oil. Known to occasionally act as a contact allergen due to manufacturing impurities like amidoamines.

name: Methylisothiazolinone
profile: A powerful synthetic biocide preservative. It is a highly potent contact allergen and sensitizer, banned in leave-on cosmetic products in the EU.

name: Diazolidinyl Urea
profile: An antimicrobial preservative. Works by slowly releasing small amounts of formaldehyde over time, posing risks of allergic reactions and contact dermatitis.

name: Imidazolidinyl Urea
profile: A formaldehyde-releasing chemical preservative. Common allergen in cosmetic creams and hair products, linked to skin sensitization.

name: Quaternium-15
profile: A preservative found in hair conditioners and soaps. Acts as a formaldehyde releaser, classified as a human allergen and skin sensitizer.

name: Benzalkonium Chloride
profile: An organic salt used as a preservative and disinfectant. Known to cause severe skin, eye, and respiratory irritation under frequent exposure.

name: Stearalkonium Chloride
profile: A cationic surfactant used in hair conditioners to prevent static. Can cause mild skin and eye irritation in high concentrations.

name: Behentrimonium Chloride
profile: A quaternary ammonium salt. Used to detangle and condition hair. Considered moderately toxic to aquatic life and can cause skin irritation.

name: Retinyl Palmitate
profile: A combination of retinol and palmitic acid. While a popular anti-aging active, it can generate free radicals when exposed to UV sunlight, so it should be used at night.

name: Homosalate
profile: An organic compound used in sunscreens. Shows evidence of endocrine-disrupting activity, mimicking estrogen and accumulating in the body.

name: Octisalate
profile: A chemical sunscreen agent. Generally well-tolerated, though it can act as a skin penetration enhancer, letting other harmful chemicals cross the dermal barrier.

name: Octocrylene
profile: A UV-absorbing agent. Known to degrade over time into benzophenone, a toxic chemical linked to hormone disruption and cell mutation.

name: Avobenzone
profile: A chemical filter providing broad UVA protection. Unstable when exposed to sunlight, requiring stabilizers, and can cause mild skin irritation.

name: Zinc Oxide
profile: A natural mineral sunscreen agent. Sits on top of the skin to block UV rays physically. Exceptionally safe, non-irritating, and reef-safe.

name: Iron Oxides
profile: Naturally occurring mineral pigments used to color cosmetics. Completely safe, non-toxic, and hypoallergenic.

name: Carbon Black
profile: A dark black pigment used in eyeliners and mascaras. Classified as a possible human carcinogen when inhaled in loose powder form.

name: Cyclopentasiloxane
profile: A volatile silicone compound. Poses environmental bioaccumulation concerns; restricted in wash-off cosmetic products in the European Union.

name: Cyclotetrasiloxane
profile: A cyclic silicone compound. Classified as an endocrine disruptor and suspected reproductive toxin in animal studies.

name: Sodium Hyaluronate
profile: The sodium salt of hyaluronic acid. A highly effective skin-identical humectant that binds moisture to the skin. Completely safe.

name: Tocopheryl Acetate
profile: A stable form of Vitamin E. Functions as an antioxidant to protect skin from free radicals and preserve oils. Extremely safe.

name: Panthenol
profile: Pro-vitamin B5. Acts as a powerful skin-soothing humectant that aids in skin barrier repair and wound healing. Completely safe.

name: Salicylic Acid
profile: A beta hydroxy acid derived from willow bark. Clears pores and treats acne. Safe for topical cosmetic use, though should be avoided in high oral doses.

name: Glycolic Acid
profile: An alpha hydroxy acid derived from sugar cane. Promotes skin cell turnover. Safe, though increases skin sensitivity to sunlight.

name: Allantoin
profile: An organic compound that soothes and protects the skin, promoting cell regeneration. Highly safe and non-irritating.

name: Sodium Dehydroacetate
profile: A sodium salt used as a preservative in cosmetics. Effective at preventing microbial growth and possesses a high safety profile.

name: Benzyl Alcohol
profile: An organic alcohol used as a preservative and fragrance component. Can act as a contact allergen for individuals with eczema or sensitive skin.

name: Sodium Lauroyl Sarcosinate
profile: An amino-acid derived foaming surfactant. Exceptionally mild and safe, used in toothpastes and shampoos as a skin-friendly alternative to sulfates.

name: Guar Hydroxypropyltrimonium Chloride
profile: A water-soluble quaternary ammonium derivative of guar gum. Detangles hair and provides smooth texture. Safe and non-toxic.

name: Pectin
profile: A structural heteropolysaccharide extracted from citrus fruits and apples. Used as a natural gelling agent in jams and jellies. Highly safe.

name: Agar Agar
profile: A natural gelatinous substance derived from red algae. Used as a plant-based vegetarian gelatin substitute. Completely safe.

name: Calcium Alginate
profile: An insoluble gelatinous substance extracted from brown algae. Used as a safe food stabilizer and firming agent.

name: Locust Bean Gum
profile: A natural vegetable gum extracted from carob tree seeds. Safe, fiber-rich, and used to thicken dairy products and dressings.

name: Gellan Gum
profile: A water-soluble polysaccharide produced by bacterial fermentation. Safe, vegetarian gelling agent used in dairy alternatives.

name: Gum Arabic
profile: A natural gum made of hardened sap from acacia trees. Used as a stabilizer in soft drinks and candies. Completely safe.

name: Sodium Hexametaphosphate
profile: A mixture of polymeric metaphosphates. Used to preserve food textures. High dietary intake can increase phosphate loads, impacting kidney health.

name: Tetrasodium Pyrophosphate
profile: A chemical compound used as a buffering agent and protein stabilizer. Moderation is advised to prevent excess phosphorus intake.

name: Disodium Phosphate
profile: A sodium salt of phosphoric acid. Common in processed cheese and evaporated milk. Elevated phosphate intake is linked to cardiovascular calcification.

name: Monopotassium Phosphate
profile: A soluble salt used as a food additive and source of potassium. Completely safe when consumed within standard dietary guidelines.

name: Sodium Propionate
profile: A sodium salt of propionic acid. Safely prevents mold growth in bakery products and does not accumulate in the body.

name: Natamycin
profile: A naturally occurring antifungal agent produced by soil bacteria. Used to prevent mold growth on cheese rinds and sausages. Safe.

name: Sorbic Acid
profile: A natural organic acid widely used as an antimicrobial food preservative. High safety profile and easily metabolized by the human body.

name: Calcium Acetate
profile: A calcium salt of acetic acid. Used to prevent mold in baking and regulate acidity. Completely safe.

name: Boric Acid
profile: A weak acid of boron. Banned as a food preservative worldwide due to systemic toxicity, hormone disruption, and accumulation in organs.

name: Neotame
profile: A high-intensity artificial sweetener derived from aspartame. Extremely sweet, under ongoing review for long-term metabolic safety.

name: Advantame
profile: An ultra-high intensity sweetener derived from aspartame and vanillin. Metabolized safely, though subject to standard sweetener regulations.

name: Thaumatin
profile: A low-calorie natural sweet protein extracted from the katemfe fruit of West Africa. Completely safe and highly potent.

name: Isomalt
profile: A sugar substitute derived from sugar beets. Has minimal impact on blood sugar, though excessive consumption can lead to laxative effects.

name: Lactitol
profile: A sugar alcohol used in low-calorie foods. Promotes dental health and has low glycemic impact, but can cause gas and bloating in high doses.

name: Mannitol
profile: A sugar alcohol sweetener and humectant. Safe for consumption, commonly used in sugar-free gum, though can cause mild digestive bloating.

name: Sodium Cocoyl Glycinate
profile: A mild amino acid-based surfactant derived from coconut. Commonly used in premium facewashes and baby soaps. Highly biodegradable and extremely gentle on the skin.

name: Potassium Iodate
profile: Used in bakery items and iodized table salt. While iodine is essential, excessive intake of potassium iodate can lead to thyroid dysfunction and is banned as a bread additive in some countries.

name: Polyglycerol Polyricinoleate (PGPR)
profile: A cheap emulsifier derived from castor bean oil, widely used in Indian chocolates to reduce cocoa butter usage. Considered safe in moderation, but high intake can cause mild digestive issues.

name: Cocoamidopropyl Betaine (CAPB)
profile: A synthetic surfactant derived from coconut oil, common in Indian facewashes and shampoos. Much gentler than SLS, but can cause contact allergies in individuals sensitive to processing impurities.

name: BHA (Butylated Hydroxyanisole)
profile: A synthetic antioxidant used to preserve oils and fats in packaged snacks and cosmetics. Classified as a possible human carcinogen by the WHO/IARC and an endocrine disruptor.

name: Guar Gum
profile: A natural seed polysaccharide extracted from the guar bean, widely cultivated in India. Used as a binder in instant noodles (like Maggi) and sauces. Highly safe and acts as a prebiotic fiber.
